questions.question_text: A sponsored licensee receives a $5,000 earnest money check after a buyer's offer is accepted on Thursday. By when must the check be deposited into the trust account?
questions.correct_answer: No later than the next business day, Friday
distractors: Within 3 business days of receipt
Within 24 hours of the offer being written
By the following Monday at close of business
questions.explanation: Illinois requires that trust funds be deposited no later than the next business day after contract acceptance. Since the offer was accepted on Thursday, the deposit deadline is Friday.

questions.question_text: A sponsored licensee opens a personal trust account at a local bank to hold client escrow funds. Under Illinois law, this arrangement is:
questions.correct_answer: Prohibited because escrow accounts must be maintained by the sponsoring broker
distractors: Permitted if the licensee notifies IDFPR within 10 days
Permitted as long as the account is at an Illinois bank
Allowed if the managing broker cosigns the account
questions.explanation: In Illinois, escrow (trust) accounts must be maintained under the control of the sponsoring broker (68 Ill. Adm. Code 1450.750). A sponsored licensee cannot independently open or manage an escrow account for client funds; all escrow moneys the licensee accepts must be placed in the sponsoring broker's escrow account.

questions.question_text: Which requirement applies to all real estate trust (escrow) accounts in Illinois?
questions.correct_answer: They must be maintained at a federally insured depository
distractors: They must be held at a financial institution located in Illinois
They must hold funds from only one transaction at a time
They require IDFPR pre-approval before opening
questions.explanation: Under 68 Ill. Adm. Code 1450.750, every escrow account, whether interest-bearing or non-interest-bearing, must be maintained at a federally insured depository. There is no requirement that the institution be located in Illinois, no one-transaction-per-account rule, and no IDFPR pre-approval requirement.

questions.question_text: A buyer's contract states that the earnest money deposit is due within 5 days of acceptance. The managing broker receives the check on day 2. When must the deposit occur?
questions.correct_answer: No later than the next business day after receipt
distractors: Per the contract terms, any time within 5 days of acceptance
Immediately upon receipt of the check
Within 3 business days of the contract date
questions.explanation: The contract controls when the buyer must deliver the earnest money, but once the sponsoring broker receives it, 68 Ill. Adm. Code 1450.750(d) requires deposit into the escrow account no later than the next business day after receipt. The broker may not hold a received check until the contract's day-5 deadline.

questions.question_text: A managing broker operates two offices and wants to maintain a single trust account for both locations. Under Illinois law, this practice is:
questions.correct_answer: Permitted because the sponsoring broker controls all trust funds
distractors: Prohibited unless each office has its own trust account
Allowed only with written IDFPR approval
Permitted only if both offices are in the same county
questions.explanation: Illinois places escrow-account responsibility on the sponsoring broker, not on individual office locations (68 Ill. Adm. Code 1450.750). A sponsoring broker may maintain one escrow account serving multiple offices, or more than one account, as long as proper records are maintained for all transactions.

questions.question_text: A managing broker keeps personal funds in the sponsoring broker's escrow account well in excess of the minimum balance the bank requires to avoid service charges. This practice is:
questions.correct_answer: A violation because personal funds may not exceed the amount needed to avoid service charges
distractors: Permitted because Illinois allows a flat $500 of personal funds
Prohibited because no personal funds may ever be kept in an escrow account
Allowed as long as the funds are withdrawn within 30 days
questions.explanation: 68 Ill. Adm. Code 1450.750 bars personal funds in an escrow account with one exception: an amount sufficient to avoid incurring service charges, documented as not exceeding the minimum the depository requires. Illinois sets no fixed dollar allowance (such as $500); anything beyond what the bank requires is impermissible commingling.

questions.question_text: A managing broker wants to place client escrow funds into an interest-bearing trust account. What must the broker obtain first?
questions.correct_answer: Written consent from all parties with funds held
distractors: Verbal agreement from both buyer and seller
A special license endorsement from the IDFPR
Written approval from the closing title company
questions.explanation: Illinois permits interest-bearing trust accounts only when the managing broker has obtained written consent from all parties whose funds are deposited in the account. Verbal consent or consent from only one party is insufficient.

questions.question_text: An IDFPR auditor arrives at a brokerage without advance notice and requests access to trust account records. The managing broker asks the auditor to return next week. The broker's request is:
questions.correct_answer: Improper because IDFPR may audit trust accounts at any time
distractors: Proper if the broker provides records within 7 days
Proper because auditors must provide 48 hours notice
Improper only if the broker is under formal investigation
questions.explanation: IDFPR has the authority to audit trust accounts at any time without prior notice. A managing broker who refuses or delays access to trust account records is in violation of Illinois license law.

questions.question_text: A managing broker closes a transaction in January 2026. How long must the trust account records for that transaction be retained?
distractors: 3 years from the transaction date
7 years from the transaction date
Until the next IDFPR audit is completed
questions.explanation: Illinois requires that trust account records be maintained for 5 years from the transaction date. This is longer than some other states and ensures records remain available for potential audits or disputes.

questions.question_text: A managing broker keeps $400 of personal funds in the trust account for bank charges and maintains separate ledgers for each client. This arrangement is:
questions.correct_answer: Permitted if the bank requires that amount to avoid service charges
distractors: Prohibited because any personal funds constitute commingling
Permitted only with annual IDFPR approval
Allowed only if the broker has fewer than 10 active clients
questions.explanation: 68 Ill. Adm. Code 1450.750 lets the sponsoring broker keep personal funds in the escrow account only in an amount sufficient to avoid incurring bank service charges, documented as not exceeding the minimum the depository requires. There is no fixed $500 allowance; the $400 here is compliant only because it is what the bank requires to avoid charges.

questions.question_text: A tenant moves out and the landlord has no damage claims. Under the Illinois Security Deposit Return Act, when must the landlord return the full security deposit?
questions.correct_answer: Within 45 days after the tenant vacates
distractors: Within 30 days after the tenant vacates
Within 14 days after the tenant vacates
Within 60 days after the tenant vacates
questions.explanation: Under the Security Deposit Return Act (765 ILCS 710/1), a landlord making no deductions must return the deposit in full within 45 days after the tenant vacates. The 30-day deadline applies only to furnishing an itemized statement of damages when the landlord withholds part of the deposit. Since January 1, 2024 (P.A. 103-224), the Act applies to all residential rental property, not just buildings with 5 or more units.

questions.question_text: A landlord deducts $800 from a tenant's security deposit for carpet damage but does not provide any documentation. Under Illinois law, the tenant may recover up to:
questions.correct_answer: Two times the security deposit amount
distractors: The amount of the deduction only
Three times the security deposit amount
One and one-half times the deposit amount
questions.explanation: Under the Security Deposit Return Act (765 ILCS 710/1), a landlord who refuses to supply the required itemized statement (or supplies it in bad faith) and fails to return the deposit due within the statutory time limits is liable for twice the amount of the security deposit due, plus court costs and reasonable attorney's fees. Since 2024 the Act covers all residential rental property.

questions.question_text: A landlord in downstate Illinois wants to charge a security deposit equal to 3 months' rent. Under statewide Illinois law, this amount is:
questions.correct_answer: Permitted because Illinois has no statewide deposit cap
distractors: Prohibited because deposits cannot exceed 2 months' rent
Allowed only for commercial leases
Permitted only if the tenant has poor credit history
questions.explanation: Illinois imposes no statewide cap on the amount a landlord may charge as a security deposit. Local law can be stricter: the Cook County RTLO caps security deposits at one and one-half months' rent in suburban Cook County. The Chicago RLTO strictly regulates how deposits are held and returned but does not cap the amount.

questions.question_text: A Chicago landlord holds a tenant's security deposit in a non-interest-bearing checking account. Under the Chicago RLTO, this practice is:
questions.correct_answer: Non-compliant because the RLTO requires an interest-bearing account
distractors: Compliant as long as the deposit is returned within 30 days
Permitted if the landlord owns fewer than 5 units
Allowed if the tenant waives interest rights in the lease
questions.explanation: The Chicago Residential Landlord and Tenant Ordinance (RLTO) requires landlords to hold security deposits in federally insured interest-bearing accounts and pay annual interest to tenants. A non-interest-bearing account violates these stricter Chicago requirements.

questions.question_text: A Chicago landlord withholds $500 from a security deposit without providing an itemized statement within the required period. Under the RLTO, the tenant may be entitled to:
questions.correct_answer: Two times the deposit plus attorney fees
distractors: Return of only the withheld $500 amount
Three times the deposit without any fees
One full month of rent as a flat penalty
questions.explanation: The Chicago RLTO imposes stricter penalties than statewide law. A landlord who violates the ordinance's security deposit provisions (Section 5-12-080(a)-(e)) is liable for damages equal to two times the security deposit plus interest, and the prevailing tenant also recovers reasonable attorney's fees under Section 5-12-180.
